3.happening: Start the list of nodes to draw.
3.why: The whole tree is shown so you can watch it shrink.

4.why: Each vertex becomes a drawn node.

5.why: The node carries its id as its label.
5.invariant: Nodes keep a fixed position for the whole run.

6.happening: Start the list of edges to draw.
6.why: Edges stay visible even after nodes are trimmed, so the structure is clear.
6.invariant: One drawn edge per input edge.

7.why: Every connection must be drawn and counted.

8.happening: Read one endpoint of the edge.
8.why: We need both ends to draw and count it.
8.invariant: a is a valid node id.

9.why: Together a and b define the edge.
9.invariant: b is a valid node id.

10.why: It stays on screen the whole run to keep the shape recognisable.

11.why: It will hold the centre node(s).
11.invariant: result ends up holding the MHT roots.

12.happening: Only {n} node(s) — this tiny tree is its own centre.
12.why: With 1 or 2 nodes there is nothing to peel; every node is a root.
12.invariant: A 1- or 2-node tree needs no trimming.

12.happening: {n} nodes — big enough to need peeling.
12.why: We must trim leaves until the centre is exposed.
12.invariant: Trees larger than 2 nodes have 1 or 2 centroids.

13.why: Each is a root of this tiny tree.

14.why: In a 1- or 2-node tree every node is a centre.

15.why: No peeling is needed for such a small tree.

16.why: We need each node's neighbours to compute and update degrees.
16.invariant: adj[i] lists every neighbour of node i.

17.why: Each needs an empty neighbour list to start.

18.happening: Give node {i} an empty neighbour list.
18.why: It fills in as edges are read.

19.why: A node's degree is what decides when it becomes a leaf.
19.invariant: degree[i] is how many edges touch node i.

20.invariant: Every node gets a degree slot.

22.happening: Loop over every edge to fill adjacency and degree.
22.why: Each edge adds a neighbour to both ends and raises both degrees.
22.invariant: Every edge is counted for both endpoints.

23.why: We update this end's neighbours and degree.
23.invariant: a is a valid node id.

24.invariant: b is a valid node id.

25.why: Adjacency must capture both directions of an undirected edge.

26.why: The edge is undirected, so it counts both ways.

27.why: One more edge touches node a.

28.why: One more edge touches node b.

29.why: 0 = active, 1 = leaf, 2 = trimmed — this drives the colours.
29.invariant: state[i] reflects node i's current role.

30.invariant: Every node gets a state slot.

32.happening: Collect the first layer of leaves.
32.why: Peeling starts from the outermost nodes — the degree-1 leaves.
32.invariant: leaves holds the current outer layer.

33.why: A leaf has exactly one neighbour.
33.invariant: Only degree-1 nodes join the layer.

34.happening: Node {i} has degree 1 — it's a leaf.
34.why: Degree-1 nodes are the outer edge of the tree.

34.happening: Node {i} has degree {degree[i]} — not a leaf yet.
34.why: Interior nodes are peeled only after their branches are gone.

35.why: It will be trimmed in this round.
35.invariant: The layer holds this round's leaves.

36.why: So it shows amber, waiting to be trimmed.

37.happening: Count all {n} nodes as still in the tree.
37.why: We peel until this drops to 2 or fewer.
37.invariant: remaining is how many untrimmed nodes are left.

38.why: The cursor lights up only while a leaf is being trimmed.

39.happening: {remaining} nodes remain — more than 2, so keep peeling.
39.why: The centre is reached only when 1 or 2 nodes are left.
39.invariant: Each round removes the entire outer leaf layer.

39.happening: Only {remaining} node(s) remain — the centre is exposed.
39.why: 1 or 2 survivors are exactly the MHT roots.

40.why: We must peel the whole layer at once, not one node.
40.invariant: count is fixed for this round before any trimming.

41.why: The whole layer disappears this round.
41.invariant: remaining shrinks by a full layer each round.

42.happening: Start at the first leaf in the layer.
42.why: Each leaf in the layer is trimmed in turn.
42.invariant: k indexes within the current layer.

43.why: Every leaf in the layer must be trimmed before the next round.
43.invariant: The layer is peeled completely each round.

43.happening: The whole layer is trimmed — start the next round.
43.why: New leaves exposed this round become the next layer.
43.invariant: The next layer is ready in leaves.

44.happening: Take leaf {leaves[0]} from the front of the layer.
44.why: Process leaves in the order they were found.

45.why: It is about to be trimmed.

46.happening: Light up leaf {leaf} as the active cursor.
46.why: So you can see exactly which leaf is being cut.
46.invariant: cur points at the leaf being trimmed.

47.why: It is peeled from the tree, but stays on screen so you see the shape shrink.

48.why: So it is never counted as a neighbour again.

49.why: Cutting the leaf lowers each surviving neighbour's degree.

50.invariant: All neighbours are checked before moving on.

51.invariant: nb is adjacent to the trimmed leaf.

52.happening: Neighbour {nb} is still in the tree — cut the edge to it.
52.why: Its degree drops by one now that this leaf is gone.

52.happening: Neighbour {nb} was already trimmed — skip it.
52.why: A trimmed node's degree is frozen at 0.
52.invariant: No node's degree is lowered twice for the same cut.

53.why: The edge to the trimmed leaf no longer counts.

54.happening: Neighbour {nb} now has degree 1 — it just became a leaf.
54.why: It joins the NEXT layer to be peeled.
54.invariant: Newly exposed leaves feed the next round.

54.happening: Neighbour {nb} still has degree {degree[nb]} — not a leaf yet.
54.why: It stays interior until more of its branches are cut.

55.happening: Add {nb} to the next leaf layer.
55.why: It will be trimmed in the following round.
55.invariant: The layer grows as new leaves appear.

56.why: So it shows amber, queued for the next round.

57.why: All neighbours of the leaf must be checked.
57.invariant: Each neighbour is visited once per trim.

58.happening: Clear the cursor — this leaf is done.
58.why: No leaf is active between trims.

59.happening: Move to the next leaf in this layer.
59.why: The whole layer is peeled before the next round.

60.happening: Rebuild the result from the survivors.
60.why: Whatever was never trimmed is the tree's centre.
60.invariant: result will hold the 1 or 2 centroids.

62.happening: Node {i} was never trimmed — it's a centre.
62.why: The centre is what remains after all leaves are peeled.

62.happening: Node {i} was trimmed — not a centre.
62.why: Peeled nodes cannot be MHT roots.

63.why: It is one of the MHT roots.
63.invariant: result holds the centroids in order.

64.why: These 1 or 2 nodes give the minimum possible tree height.
64.invariant: The centre of a tree is its set of MHT roots.

init.happening: Drawing the tree, computing degrees, and collecting the first leaf layer.
init.why: We need the full structure and every degree before peeling.

check.happening: Testing a node's degree or its trimmed state.
check.why: Degree decides leaf-hood; state decides survival.
check.invariant: No node changes on a pure check line.

loop.happening: Iterating over nodes, leaves, or neighbours.
loop.why: Peeling walks whole layers and each leaf's neighbours.
loop.invariant: Each item is visited in order.

update.happening: Trimming a leaf, lowering a degree, or exposing a new leaf.
update.why: These are the state changes that shrink the tree.
update.invariant: A trimmed node stays dimmed on screen.

move.happening: Advancing to the next leaf or neighbour, or lighting the cursor.
move.why: The peel walks the current layer node by node.

return.happening: Collecting and returning the surviving centre node(s).
return.why: Peeling has reached the tree's centroid(s).
return.invariant: The survivors are the MHT roots.
